1: Bildad delivers his second speech (his first was in Job 8). Like Eliphaz’s second speech, it is more aggressive than the first. Where Bildad’s first speech appealed to natural and generational cycles (Job 8:8–19), his second speech constructs an elaborate fate-of-the-wicked poem (vv5–21). It is the most sustained and vivid poetic treatment of the wicked man’s doom in the dialogue section, drawing on a range of ANE literary conventions: hunting-trap imagery, lamp-extinction symbolism, death-personification, and name-erasure curses.
2: “How long before you stop talking? Think first, and then we will have our say.” ʿaḏ-ʾānāh tiśîmûn qinṣê-millîn (how long will you set snares of words?) — the plural tiśîmûn suggests Bildad may be addressing Job and his supporters, or addressing the company collectively. Tāḇînû (understand/discern first) before speaking — the wisdom-pedagogy principle inverted: Bildad accuses Job of speaking without prior understanding. The two-step (ûḇaʿᵃḵēn nᵉḏabbēr, then we will speak) implies a proper order: comprehension before articulation. Bildad is positioning his own speech as properly understood, in contrast to Job’s reflexive protest.
3: “Why do you regard us as animals? Why are we worthless in your eyes?” Bildad’s personal pique: Job’s dismissal of the Friends as animals (nibᵉḏamennû, as animals) and as defiled/worthless (ṭāmîm bᵉʿênêḵem). Job has in fact compared himself favorably to the Friends’ wisdom (Job 12:7–9: the animals and birds know more than the Friends know), which Bildad apparently took personally. The breakdown of the discourse community — from respectful debate to mutual contempt — is tracked in these accusation-counter-accusation exchanges that mark the second cycle.
4: “You who tear yourself apart in your rage — will the earth be forsaken on your account? Will the rock be moved from its place for you?” Ṭōrēp napšô bᵉʾappô (you who tear your own soul in your anger) — Bildad turns Job’s warrior-God imagery back on Job himself: the tearing is self-inflicted, the product of Job’s own rage. The rhetorical questions — will the earth be abandoned on your account? will the rock be moved for you? — mockingly suggest that Job believes the entire natural order should accommodate his complaints. The rock (ṣûr) as foundational stability recalls the creation theology in which God set the foundations immovably (Job 38:4–7).
5: “Yes, the light of the wicked is extinguished; the flame of his fire gives no light.” The lamp-extinction motif begins the fate-of-the-wicked poem. Nēr rᵉšāʿîm yidʿāḵ (the lamp of the wicked is extinguished) — in the ancient world, the lamp (nēr) was both a practical light source and a symbol of life, family continuity, and the presence of the divine. To have one’s lamp extinguished was to have one’s life and line ended (Proverbs 13:9: “the lamp of the wicked will be extinguished”; Job 20:20; Job 24:20). The flame that gives no light (šabaḥ ʾēšô nōgāh) — even what appears to burn is without illuminating effect.
6: “The light in his tent grows dark, and the lamp over him is extinguished.” The movement from communal lamp (v5) to personal/domestic lamp (v6): ʾôr ḥāšaḵ bᵉʾōhᵃlô (the light darkens in his tent) and wᵉnērô ʿālāyw yiddaʿᵃḵ (his lamp above him is extinguished). The tent (ʾōhel) and the lamp-above-him are domestic images. In patriarchal and nomadic contexts, the well-lit tent was a sign of hospitality, prosperity, and protection; the dark tent is its opposite — abandonment and curse. The imagery applies to Job’s situation ironically: Job’s household has already been darkened (Job 1:18–19).
7: “His powerful stride is shortened, and his own schemes bring him down.” Yiṣṣᵃrû ṣᵃʿᵃdê ʾônô (the steps of his strength are shortened) — the diminishment of physical power. Wᵉtašlîḵēhû ʿᵃṣāṯô (his own counsel/plans throw him down) — the self-entrapment motif: the wicked man’s downfall is engineered by his own scheming. This is the boomerang principle of Proverbs (Proverbs 1:18–19; Job 26:27; Psalms 7:15–16): the trap the wicked set for others catches themselves. The transition from lamp imagery to stride-shortening to self-entrapment prepares the extensive trap-and-net imagery of vv8–10.
8: “He is thrown into a net by his own feet, and he walks right onto a snare.” Kî šullaḥ bᵉreṯeṯ bᵉraglāyw (for he is sent into a net by his own feet) — the passive suggests divine agency behind the snare. The extensive trap vocabulary in vv8–10 — rešeṯ (net), śᵉḇāḵāh (snare), pāḥ (trap), ṣāmîm (snare), ḥeḇel (rope/cord) — is a deliberate accumulation of hunting imagery. In the ANE, hunting provided one of the primary metaphors for divine judgment (Ezekiel 12:13; Job 17:20; Jeremiah 18:22; Hosea 7:12). The multiplication of trap-types implies inescapability: however the wicked man moves, a trap awaits.
9: “A trap catches him by the heel; a snare holds him tight.” Yōʾḥez bᵉʿāqēḇ pāḥ (a trap seizes him by the heel) — the heel (ʿāqēḇ) as the point of vulnerability. The heel is the anatomically vulnerable site in ancient combat and is associated in the OT with Jacob/Israel (Genesis 25:26: grasping the heel) and with the serpent’s attack (Genesis 3:15: striking the heel). Yᵃḥᵃzēq ʿālāyw ṣāmîm (the snare holds him firm) — the trap’s grip is total. The hunting imagery in vv8–10 may have influenced Psalms 91:3 (“he will save you from the fowler’s snare”) as the positive counter-promise.
10: “A rope is buried in the ground for him, and a trap waits on his path.” Ṭāmûn bāʾāreṣ ḥaḇlô (his rope is hidden in the ground) — the concealed trap, the hidden danger. The trap waits on the nᵉṯîḇāh (path, way) — the path-language ties the hunting imagery to the wisdom literature’s two-ways theology: the wicked man’s very path is mined with traps. Compare Psalms 1:6 (“the way of the wicked will perish”) — not through external intervention alone but through the structural entrapment that the wicked way entails. The “way” that leads to death is a trapped way.
11: “Terrors frighten him on every side and push him forward at every step.” Sābîḇ bittᵃhûhû bālāhôṯ (terrors frighten him on every side) — the plural bālāhôṯ (terrors, sudden destructions) are personified forces surrounding the wicked man. Wᵉhēpîṣuhû lᵉraglāyw (they push/scatter him at his feet) — the terror-forces drive him forward, not allowing him to stand. The image is of a panicked animal driven by beaters on all sides — no matter which direction the wicked man moves, terror drives him further into the trap. The “king of terrors” (v14) is the destination toward which these surrounding terrors drive him.
12: “His strength is starved, and disaster stands ready at his side.” Yᵉhî-rāʿēb ʾônô (his strength is hungry/starved) — the strength (ʾôn) that made his stride powerful (v7) is now famished. Wᵉʾêḏ nāḵôn lᵉṣalʿô (and disaster stands ready at his side) — ʾêḏ (disaster, calamity) as a quasi-personified attendant: not a random misfortune but a constant companion ready to strike. The image of disaster as a constant companion at the side contrasts with Psalms 23:6 (“goodness and steadfast love will follow me all my days”) — for the wicked, the constant follower is ʾêḏ rather than ḥeseḏ.
13: “His skin is eaten away piece by piece; the firstborn of death devours his body.” Yōʾkal baḏḏê ʿôrô (it eats his skin piece by piece) — a disease that consumes the body from outside. Yōʾkal baḏḏāyw bᵉḵôr māweṯ (the firstborn of death devours his limbs) — bᵉḵôr māweṯ (firstborn of death) is unique in the OT: a metaphorical or mythological figure, the most powerful of death’s offspring. In Canaanite mythology, Mot (Death) was a deity with a household and realm; his “firstborn” would be his most powerful offspring. The verse applies to Job’s condition exactly — his skin has been consumed (Job 2:7: “painful sores from the soles of his feet to the crown of his head”).
14: “His confidence is ripped away from his home, and he is brought before the king of terrors.” Yinnāteq mēʾōhᵃlô miḇṭaḥô (his confidence is torn/ripped from his tent) — miḇṭāḥ (confidence, security, what one trusts in) is what Eliphaz said was available to the righteous (Job 4:6) but the wicked lose. Wᵉtaṣʿidēhû lᵉmelek ballāhôṯ (and he is marched before the king of terrors) — melek ballāhôṯ (king of terrors) is the most vivid death-personification in the book. In Ugaritic texts, Mot is a royal figure who rules the underworld. The formal march (tṣʿd, to cause to march/step) before this king is a court procession — the wicked man is brought as a condemned prisoner before death’s throne. Revelation 1:18 responds: “I hold the keys of Death and Hades.”
15: “Something alien has moved into his tent; brimstone is scattered over his home.” Tiškon bᵉʾōhᵃlô mibbᵉlî-lô (something dwells in his tent that is not his) — the tent occupied by an alien presence, the opposite of the prosperous household. Wᵉzōreh ʿal-nāwêhû goprit (brimstone is scattered over his dwelling) — brimstone (goprit) is the material of divine judgment: the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19:24), the judgment oracles of the prophets (Isaiah 34:9; Ezekiel 38:22; Psalms 11:6). The scattering of brimstone over the dwelling makes the site permanently consecrated to destruction — nothing will grow there again (Deuteronomy 29:23).
16: “His roots dry up below; his branches wither above.” The arboreal metaphor for human life and posterity: mittaḥaṯ šāreśāyw yiḇāšû (his roots dry up below) and ûmimmaʿal yimmāl qᵉṣîrô (his branches/harvest wither above). Job used the same tree image to express hope (Job 14:7–9: “there is hope for a tree — if it is cut down it will sprout again”). Bildad denies that hope for the wicked: the roots fail before the branches wither, meaning there is no possibility of recovery. The comprehensive destruction — roots and branches — eliminates both past (roots) and future (branches) simultaneously.
17: “His memory vanishes from the land; he has no name in the open places.” Ziḵrô ʾāḇaḏ min-ʾāreṣ (his memory perishes from the earth) and wᵉlōʾ-šēm lô ʿal-pᵉnê-ḥûṣ (no name for him in the open/outside) — the erasure of name and memory is the ultimate death in the ancient world, where the name’s survival constituted a form of immortality. The name that persisted in the public places (ḥûṣ = streets, public squares) was the sign of a life that had mattered. Proverbs 10:7 and 22:1 make the name’s persistence a mark of the righteous. To have no name in the open places is the final deletion from communal memory.
18: “He is driven from light into darkness and banished from the world.” Yihdᵃpēhû miʾôr ʾel-ḥōšeḵ (he is thrust from light into darkness) — the light-darkness contrast that runs through the book reaches its destination here: the wicked man arrives at the permanent darkness that was his trajectory from Job 15:22. Ûmittēḇēl yᵉniddūhû (and from the world he is banished) — tēḇēl (world, inhabited earth) is the sphere of human community and divine ordering. Banishment from the tēḇēl is cosmic exile — not just social death or personal death but exclusion from the ordered world itself. Compare Cain’s banishment (Genesis 4:12–14) as the prototype of this condition.
19: “He has no offspring or descendants among his people, and no survivor where he once lived.” Lōʾ-nîn lô wᵉlōʾ-neḵeḏ bᵉʿammô (no son and no grandson among his people) — the genealogical erasure following the name-erasure of v17. Wᵉʾên śārîḏ bimgûrāyw (no survivor in his dwelling places) — the word śārîḏ (survivor, refugee) was used of those who survived divine judgment (Jeremiah 42:17; Job 44:14; Joel 2:32). The complete absence of any survivor from the wicked man’s household is the absolute form of the divine judgment that leaves nothing remaining. Bildad’s description of total annihilation is implicitly applied to Job: no children (Job 1:18–19), no household (Job 1:15–17).
20: “Those who come after him are horrified by his fate; those who went before him are seized with dread.” ʿal-yômô nāšammû ʾaḥᵃrōnîm (at his day those who come after are horrified) and wᵉqaḏmōnîm ʾāḥᵃzû śāʿar (and those who went before are seized with dread) — the judgment functions as a warning to all generations: past and future witnesses are both appalled. This universalizing gesture frames the fate-of-the-wicked poem as an instructional text for all observers. The pedagogical function of observed judgment is a key mechanism of wisdom ethics (Proverbs 24:30–34; Psalms 37:35–36).
21: Bildad’s summary verdict closes the poem with the explicit category: lōʾ-yāḏaʿ ʾēl (the one who does not know God). In the wisdom and prophetic traditions, “not knowing God” is the fundamental orientation that produces all the downstream consequences the poem has described (Jeremiah 2:8; Job 4:22; Job 9:3,6; Hosea 4:1,6). Not-knowing is both cognitive (lacking accurate understanding of who God is) and relational (refusing the covenant-relationship with God). Bildad’s implicit accusation: Job’s conduct reveals him to be one who does not know God — the exact opposite of the book’s opening claim (Job 1:1: “he feared God”).
